low.title: ✅ LOW RISK PREGNANCY
low.message: Your maternal health assessment shows no significant risk indicators. You are on a healthy pregnancy path — keep up the excellent work with your prenatal routine.
recommendations: Maintain a balanced diet rich in iron, calcium, and folate
Stay hydrated — aim for 8–10 glasses of water daily
Get 7–9 hours of sleep each night
Engage in light physical activity such as walking or prenatal yoga
warning_signs: Sudden swelling of hands, face, or feet

eat: Leafy greens (spinach, fenugreek) for iron and folate
Dairy products (milk, paneer, curd) for calcium
Lentils and legumes for protein
Fresh seasonal fruits for vitamins
Whole grains (brown rice, oats) for sustained energy
avoid: Raw or undercooked meat and eggs
Exercises lying flat on back after 20 weeks

1.label: 1st Trimester (Weeks 1–13)
tips: Start folic acid 400mcg/day immediately to prevent neural tube defects
Schedule your first prenatal visit and blood tests
Manage morning sickness with small frequent meals and ginger tea
Avoid alcohol, tobacco, and unprescribed medications entirely
Get a dental checkup — hormonal changes affect gum health

2.label: 2nd Trimester (Weeks 14–27)
tips: Track fetal movements from week 18–20 onwards
Complete the anomaly scan (18–20 weeks) as scheduled
Begin pelvic floor (Kegel) exercises
Discuss birth plan options with your doctor
Monitor weight gain — aim for 0.3–0.5 kg/week

3.label: 3rd Trimester (Weeks 28–42)
tips: Attend weekly or bi-weekly check-ups as advised
Monitor fetal kick counts daily (at least 10 kicks in 2 hours)
Prepare hospital bag and birth plan
Learn warning signs of preterm labor
Rest on your left side to improve blood flow to baby

moderate.title: ⚠️ MODERATE RISK PREGNANCY
moderate.message: Some risk indicators have been identified. Increased vigilance and more frequent monitoring are recommended to ensure both your and your baby's safety.
recommendations: Increase prenatal visit frequency to every 2 weeks
Monitor blood pressure at home twice daily
Report any unusual symptoms to your doctor within 24 hours
Reduce physical exertion and get more rest
warning_signs: Persistent headaches not relieved by rest
Sudden swelling of face, hands, or ankles
Pain below the ribs on the right side
Sudden weight gain of more than 1 kg in a week

eat: Low-sodium foods to manage blood pressure
Potassium-rich foods (banana, coconut water, sweet potato)
Magnesium-rich foods (almonds, dark chocolate, whole grains)
High-protein meals (eggs, lentils, lean meat) to protect kidneys
Fresh vegetables and fruits for antioxidants

avoid: Exercises with sudden movements or jumps
Exercise in hot or humid environments

1.label: 1st Trimester (Weeks 1–13)
tips: Inform your doctor of all risk factors (family history, prior complications) at the first visit
Begin low-dose aspirin only if recommended by your doctor
Get a full blood panel including thyroid function
Manage nausea carefully — dehydration worsens risk factors
Avoid over-the-counter medications without doctor approval

2.label: 2nd Trimester (Weeks 14–27)
tips: Complete detailed anatomy scan and cervical length check
Begin home blood pressure log — record morning and evening readings
Reduce work stress and increase rest periods during the day
Discuss corticosteroid therapy with your doctor if preterm risk exists
Track urine protein — use dipstick tests at home if advised

3.label: 3rd Trimester (Weeks 28–42)
tips: Visit your doctor every 1–2 weeks or as directed
Monitor fetal kick counts diligently
Discuss induction timeline and delivery method with your doctor
Avoid travel, especially by air, after 32 weeks
Prepare an emergency contact list and know your nearest hospital

high.title: 🚨 HIGH RISK PREGNANCY
high.message: Elevated risk factors have been detected. Immediate medical consultation and detailed preeclampsia screening are essential. Please do not delay seeking care.
recommendations: Do not wait for your next scheduled appointment
Go to emergency if you have severe headache, vision changes, or chest pain
Complete bed rest until assessed by a doctor
Avoid salt, stress, and physical exertion entirely
warning_signs: Severe headache that won't go away
Vision changes: blurring, flashes, double vision
Sudden severe swelling of face and hands

eat: Small, frequent meals every 2–3 hours
High-protein foods (eggs, paneer, dal) to support kidney function
Antioxidant-rich foods (berries, tomatoes, broccoli)
Calcium-rich foods (dairy, tofu, sesame seeds)
Plenty of fluids — water and coconut water
avoid: All added salt and sodium-heavy foods

recommended: Complete bed rest or minimal movement as advised by doctor
Gentle leg exercises while lying down to prevent clots
Position changes every 1–2 hours to improve circulation
avoid: All forms of aerobic or strenuous exercise
Standing for more than a few minutes

1.label: 1st Trimester (Weeks 1–13)
tips: Request an urgent referral to a maternal-fetal medicine (MFM) specialist
Begin low-dose aspirin (75–150mg) if not already prescribed — proven to reduce preeclampsia risk
Get a full kidney function panel, liver enzymes, and urine protein test
Inform all family members of your high-risk status
Arrange for home nursing support or caregiver assistance

2.label: 2nd Trimester (Weeks 14–27)
tips: MFM specialist visits every 1–2 weeks minimum
Uterine artery Doppler ultrasound to assess placental blood flow
24-hour urine protein collection test may be required
Begin planning for potential preterm delivery if risk escalates
Corticosteroids may be prescribed to mature fetal lungs — discuss with doctor

3.label: 3rd Trimester (Weeks 28–42)
tips: Hospital admission may be required for intensive monitoring
Non-stress tests (NST) twice weekly minimum
Delivery may be recommended before 37 weeks — discuss risks and benefits
Have your emergency bag packed and hospital route planned
Know your magnesium sulfate and antihypertensive medication instructions

low.title: ✅ PREECLAMPSIA RISK: LOW
low.message: Your assessment shows minimal indicators for preeclampsia. Continue routine prenatal care with your healthcare team.
low.explanation: Low preeclampsia risk means your blood pressure, protein levels, and other markers are within normal range. Continue attending all scheduled check-ups to maintain this status throughout pregnancy.
do: Attend all routine prenatal appointments
Monitor blood pressure at home weekly
Maintain healthy weight gain as advised
Stay active with light exercise
Take prescribed prenatal supplements
Stay well-hydrated throughout the day
dont: Skip prenatal appointments even if feeling well

eat: Calcium-rich foods (dairy, fortified plant milks)
Magnesium-rich foods (nuts, seeds, whole grains)
Omega-3 fatty acids (fatty fish, flaxseeds, walnuts)
Fresh fruits and vegetables high in vitamin C and E
Adequate protein to support placental health

1.label: 1st Trimester (Weeks 1–13)
tips: Record your baseline blood pressure for future comparison
Ensure folic acid and vitamin D supplementation is started
Avoid environmental toxins — secondhand smoke, chemical exposure
Discuss family history of preeclampsia with your doctor
Get a baseline kidney function and urine protein test

2.label: 2nd Trimester (Weeks 14–27)
tips: Complete the mid-pregnancy anatomy scan and placenta check
Begin tracking blood pressure readings at home
Increase dietary calcium to 1000mg/day
Report any new swelling or visual changes immediately
Discuss aspirin therapy with your doctor as a preventive measure

3.label: 3rd Trimester (Weeks 28–42)
tips: Blood pressure check at every visit — do not miss any
Watch for rapid weight gain (more than 1kg in a week)
Monitor urine for protein using dipstick if prescribed
Know when to go to the emergency room immediately
Discuss your delivery plan and expected timing with your doctor

moderate.title: ⚠️ PREECLAMPSIA RISK: MODERATE
moderate.message: Some indicators suggest elevated risk for preeclampsia. Close monitoring and lifestyle adjustments are essential to prevent escalation.
moderate.explanation: Moderate preeclampsia risk means one or more markers — blood pressure, protein levels, or clinical factors — are outside the ideal range. With proper care and monitoring, many moderate-risk cases are successfully managed without complications.
do: Monitor blood pressure twice daily — morning and evening
Reduce sodium intake to under 1500mg/day
Increase water intake to 10+ glasses daily
Track symptoms in a daily diary
Rest on your left side for at least 1 hour after meals
Take all prescribed medications without skipping
Attend prenatal visits every 1–2 weeks
dont: Skip prenatal appointments or blood pressure checks
Consume high-salt, pickled, or processed foods
Ignore persistent headaches or sudden swelling
Stand or sit for prolonged periods without leg elevation

eat: Low-sodium whole foods — cook from scratch when possible
Potassium-rich foods to balance BP (bananas, avocados, sweet potatoes)
Lean protein (fish, eggs, lentils) to maintain albumin levels
High-fiber foods for gut health and weight management
Calcium-fortified foods or supplements as prescribed
avoid: Table salt, pickles, papads, canned soups

1.label: 1st Trimester (Weeks 1–13)
tips: Begin low-dose aspirin (75–150mg/day) if prescribed — strongest evidence for prevention
Start calcium supplementation (1000–1500mg/day) if dietary intake is low
Get full baseline labs: renal function, liver enzymes, CBC, urine protein
Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) — harmful in pregnancy
Inform your employer of your at-risk status if physical work is involved

2.label: 2nd Trimester (Weeks 14–27)
tips: Uterine artery Doppler at 20–24 weeks to assess placental blood flow
24-hour urine collection may be ordered to quantify protein loss
Home BP log every morning and evening — share readings at each visit
Discuss antihypertensive therapy options with your doctor if BP rises
Fetal growth scan every 4 weeks to monitor baby's development

3.label: 3rd Trimester (Weeks 28–42)
tips: Non-stress tests (NST) weekly from 34 weeks onwards
Delivery at 37–38 weeks may be recommended — discuss pros and cons
Know the emergency signs that require immediate hospitalization
Have a clear plan for who will accompany you and transport options
Postpartum BP monitoring is essential — preeclampsia can develop after delivery too

high.title: 🚨 HIGH RISK: PREECLAMPSIA DETECTED
high.message: Your profile shows strong indicators for preeclampsia. Immediate medical attention is required. Please contact your doctor or go to the emergency room without delay.
high.explanation: Preeclampsia is a serious pregnancy complication characterised by high blood pressure, protein in urine, and potential organ damage. If left untreated it can progress to eclampsia (seizures), HELLP syndrome, placental abruption, or stillbirth. The only definitive cure is delivery, but expert management before delivery is critical.
do: Go to hospital or emergency room TODAY — do not wait
Monitor blood pressure every 4–6 hours at home until you can see a doctor
Rest completely on your left side
Schedule an urgent appointment within 24 hours at minimum
Take all prescribed antihypertensive medications as directed
Keep a symptom diary with time-stamps
Have someone with you at all times
dont: Engage in any physical activity beyond minimal movement
Ignore severe headaches, vision changes, or chest pain — these are emergencies
Skip medication doses under any circumstances
Consume processed foods, salt, or sodium-heavy meals
Drive yourself — arrange for someone else to drive
Delay care hoping symptoms will resolve on their own

eat: Bland, low-sodium home-cooked meals only
Small portions every 2–3 hours — large meals raise BP
High-protein foods to protect kidney and liver function
Calcium-rich foods or supplements as prescribed
Plenty of water and oral rehydration fluids if sweating or vomiting
avoid: All added salt — use herbs and lemon instead
Preserved, canned, or restaurant food entirely

immediate_actions: Call your doctor or midwife right now and describe your symptoms
If BP ≥ 160/110 or you have severe symptoms — go to emergency room immediately
Take prescribed antihypertensive medication if available and advised
Lie on your left side and avoid sudden movements
Have someone stay with you and monitor your condition
Bring your prenatal records and medication list to the hospital

1.label: 1st Trimester (Weeks 1–13)
tips: Immediate referral to maternal-fetal medicine (MFM) specialist — do not delay
Begin low-dose aspirin today if not already prescribed
Full renal and hepatic function panel, CBC, and coagulation studies required
Discuss early pregnancy termination risks vs. continuation risks with specialist
Arrange for daily home nursing visits or hospital day-care monitoring

2.label: 2nd Trimester (Weeks 14–27)
tips: Hospital admission is strongly advised for intensive monitoring
Biophysical profile (BPP) and Doppler flows weekly minimum
Corticosteroid injections to mature fetal lungs if preterm delivery anticipated
Magnesium sulfate prophylaxis may be started — do not refuse
Delivery decision before 34 weeks must balance fetal maturity vs. maternal risk

3.label: 3rd Trimester (Weeks 28–42)
tips: Delivery is the treatment — your doctor will advise the earliest safe timing
Non-stress tests daily or twice daily in hospital
Magnesium sulfate infusion during labour to prevent eclampsia
C-section may be necessary — consent and preparation in advance
Postpartum monitoring for at least 72 hours — BP can spike after delivery
